# Why we should keep the take-home exercise

We have been debating, again, whether to drop the four-hour take-home from
our backend interview loop. Several engineers have argued that the exercise
discourages strong candidates — particularly those with caring responsibilities
or current full-time jobs — and that we could replace it with a live pairing
session of similar duration. I want to push back, carefully, on that proposal.

The data we have from the last eighteen months is suggestive. Of the
twenty-three engineers we hired during that period, every single one cleared
the take-home with a score of seven or higher on our internal rubric. Of the
six people who left within their first year, four scored exactly seven; the
two strongest performers on the team a year in both scored a perfect ten.
The pattern, while not statistically airtight on a sample this small, is
consistent with what we already believed: the take-home is a meaningful
signal, and the signal sharpens as the score climbs.

The argument for replacing it with live pairing usually rests on equity
grounds. I take that seriously. But pairing has its own equity problems —
candidates who interview frequently are simply better at performing under
observation, and that skill correlates poorly with the work we actually do
day-to-day. The take-home, whatever its faults, lets a candidate think
quietly, look things up, and produce something that resembles what they
would produce on the job. That is the whole point of the exercise.

It is also worth saying that the candidates who decline the take-home tend,
in our recruiter's experience, to be candidates who would have struggled in
the role for unrelated reasons. We do not have hard numbers on this, but the
recruiter has been tracking it informally and the pattern is consistent.

So: the take-home produces hires who perform well, screens out candidates
who would have struggled, and avoids the observation-bias that pairing
introduces. We should keep it.
